lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The ambulance bay at two in the morning is a different country than the one she entered four hours ago.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Then: lights, voices, the particular sound of gurneys on wet asphalt, radios talking over each other in the dark.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Now: one rig idling at the far end. Hers. The bay doors open to the street and the street is empty and the cold comes in like something that has been waiting.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Dana Voss crosses the bay the way she has crossed a thousand bays. Not slowly. Not quickly. The way you move when your body knows where it's going and your mind is still somewhere else.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She has the transfer paperwork in her jacket pocket. Signed, countersigned, filed in the system the moment she handed it over. Everything accounted for. Everything in its place.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The bay smells of exhaust and disinfectant and the particular cold of February concrete, and under all of it something else she doesn't name.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: At the threshold between the bay and the street she stops.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Not because of a sound. Not because someone called her name. There was no sound. There was no name.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She stops the way you stop when something in your chest makes a small, involuntary movement and you need a second before you trust your face again.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The rig idles. The street is empty. Somewhere behind her, through the interior doors and the long corridor and the double swing of bay one, a hospital continues to run.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She has not looked back. This is not new. This is the same thing she has been doing for three years and two months, and she is very good at it by now, and goodness at a thing does not make it easier, only cleaner.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The cold takes her immediately, the way cold does — without negotiation, without apology. Her breath goes white in the air ahead of her and then disperses.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She walks to the rig. She opens the door. She climbs in.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The door closes with the specific sound of a door that has closed before, that will close again, that closes the same way every time regardless of what is happening on either side of it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Through the windshield: the ambulance bay. The bay doors. The threshold she just crossed. Empty, all of it, and lit the way hospitals are lit — completely, indifferently, without rest.
